channel.title: Home on Kapchits
channel.description: Recent content in Home on Kapchits

item.title: About
item.description: About Biography Georgy L. Kapchits was born on October 2, 1939. In 1967, he graduated from the Institute of Asian and African Studies at Moscow State University, majoring in Somali and Amharic languages. From 1968, he worked at the Institute of Linguistic Studies of the Russian Academy of Sciences, defending his Candidate dissertation in 1975 and his Doctorate in Philology in 1990. His main research areas include Somali syntax, temporal lexicon, kinship terminology, and the collection of Somali proverbs and folklore. He participated in more than ten international Africanist conferences and led multiple grant projects in Russia and abroad. Doctoral Degree Doctor of Philology, Certificate No. 1234-VAK, issued on April 10, 1990 by the Higher Attestation Commission of the USSR. Download PDF of the certificate External Mentions

item.title: Conferences
item.description:
Conferences Dr. Kapchits regularly presents at international conferences. Selected presentations:

XX Moscow Congress of Africanists (March 15, 2025) Title: “Modern Trends in Somali Syntax” Abstract: Overview of recent syntactic models and field data. Video: [Embed YouTube link] International Symposium “Language, Culture, and Power” (May 21, 2019) Title: “Proverbs as Tools of Social Critique” Abstract: Analysis of political metaphors in Somali proverbs. Somali Studies Conference, London (July 12, 2018) Title: “Kinship Terms and Identity in Somali Diaspora” Abstract: How kinship designations structure community ties abroad.

item.title: Contact
item.description:
Contact Contact Dr. Georgy L. Kapchits Email: kapchits@example.com Phone: +7 (495) 123-45-67 Office Address: Institute of Linguistic Studies, Moscow, Leninsky Prospect 32, Building 3 Feedback Form:

Name*

Email*

Subject

Message*

What is 3 + 4?*

Send

item.title: Course Syllabus
item.description:
Course Syllabus “Introduction to Somali Language and Culture” Course Description: An overview of Somali phonology, morphology, syntax, and sociolinguistic context. Includes readings in both Somali and English. Weekly Schedule:

Week 1: Alphabet, orthography, and phonetics. Week 2: Nominal morphology (gender, number, case). Week 3: Verb morphology and tense-aspect-mood. … Week 12: Oral tradition and proverbs study.

Recommended Readings:

Kapchits, G.L. (1980). “Syntax of the Somali Language.” Moscow University Press. Kapchits, G.L. (2005). “Somali Proverbs: Collection and Commentary.” Lewis, I.M. (2000). “A Grammatical Sketch of Somali.”

item.title: Faq
item.description: FAQ Q: Why are some pages available only in English? A: Not all content has been fully translated. If a page is missing in Russian or Somali, you will be prompted to switch to the English version. Q: How do I find the list of Somali proverbs? A: Go to Publications → Proverbs for a full table of proverbs with translations and commentary. Q: Can I download the doctoral dissertation? A: Yes, visit Documents → Dissertations and click the PDF link. Q: How can I contact Dr. Kapchits? A: See Contact for email, phone, and office address details. Q: How to participate in upcoming conferences? A: Check Research → Conferences for dates, topics, and registration information.

item.title: Grammar
item.description:
Grammar & Syntax Dr. Kapchits’s research on Somali syntax includes:

Sentence Structure: Analysis of SOV (Subject-Object-Verb) order, topic–focus particles, and case marking. Morphological Features: Exploration of singular/plural forms, definiteness markers, and gender distinctions. Clausal Relations: Use of relative clauses, subordination, and coordination in Somali narratives.

Syntax Examples:

“Ninka ayaa libaax dilay.” Literal: “Man has lion killed.” Free translation: “The man killed the lion.” “Gabadhii ay ardayda ugu fiicneyd.” Literal: “Girl that students most best.” Free translation: “The girl who was the best among the students.”

item.title: Kinship Terms
item.description:
Kinship Terms Below is a selection of Somali and Oromo kinship terminology documented by Dr. Kapchits:

Somali TermEnglish EquivalentNotes aabafatherAlso used honorifically as uncle. hooyomotherOften appears in compound kin terms. walaalsiblingGender-neutral term: brother/sister. abtimaternal uncleLiterally “mother’s brother.”

Oromo TermEnglish EquivalentNotes abbaafatherRoot for paternal lineage terms. haadhamotherSometimes pluralized for “mothers.” obboleessabrotherUsed also for “friend” in colloquial. ejauncleGeneral term for father’s brother.

item.title: Lexicon
item.description:
Lexical Glossaries

Somali TermPart of SpeechEnglish GlossNotes geeljirenouncamel herderAlso implies pastoralist. baqonounfloodingOften used metaphorically for crisis. huwatoverbto protectRoot: h-w-t. Needs suffix for tense. ilmadunounchildDerived from “ilm” (child).

item.title: Links
item.description:
Links A curated list of external resources on Somali language, culture, and general African studies: Somali-specific resources

SomaliNet.com – News and discussion forums. Somali Academy – Official language standards and publications.

African studies

African Studies Center, Boston University – Research projects and journals. Institute of African Studies, University of Ghana – Academic programs and archives.

Educational resources

Ethnologue: Somali – Language profile and statistics. Omniglot: Somali – Writing system and orthography guide.

item.title: Photos
item.description:
Photos Below is a gallery of selected photographs from field expeditions and academic presentations. Click a thumbnail to view the full-size image with a caption.

1972 Fieldwork in Hargeisa Photo: “Kapchits interviewing elders” 1985 Conference in Cairo Photo: “Panel discussion on Cushitic languages” 2005 Proverbs Publication Launch Photo: “Author at Moscow Book Fair”

item.title: Privacy Policy
item.description:
Privacy Policy This Privacy Policy outlines how Georgy L. Kapchits (“we”, “our”) collects, uses, and protects your personal information when you visit kapchits.online. Information We Collect

Personal data you provide when you fill out forms (name, email, message). Technical data (IP address, browser type, device information, and usage statistics) via cookies/log files.

Use of Information

To respond to inquiries and send requested materials. To improve our website functionality and user experience. To comply with legal obligations (e.g., GDPR for EU users).

item.title: Projects
item.description:
Projects & Grants Dr. Kapchits has led multiple research projects funded by Russian and international agencies:

“Somali Language Documentation” (Grant № RU-2010-47, 2010–2012) Funding: Russian Science Foundation Goal: Create annotated corpus of Somali oral narratives. Outcome: Digital archive of 200+ transcripts, available upon request. “Kinship and Social Structure in Somali Clans” (Grant № RU-2015-21, 2015–2018) Funding: Russian Humanitarian Science Foundation Goal: Document kinship terms and marriage customs in Hargeisa region. Outcome: Monograph (2018), database of kinship terms (150+ entries).

item.title: Scholars
item.description:
Scholars Dr. Kapchits supervised and collaborated with many field researchers. Below are summaries of notable expeditions:

Expedition to Somali Region (1972) Participants: Dr. Kapchits, A. Abdullahi, S. Mohammed Focus: Collecting oral narratives; photographing clan gatherings. Download PDF of field report Joint Conference in San Diego (2007) Paper: “Patterns of Variation in Somali Dialects” by Kapchits and colleagues. Slides: Download PowerPoint Audio: MP3 Archive

item.title: Supervisor Contact
item.description:
Supervisor Contact To apply for academic supervision with Dr. Georgy Kapchits, please follow these steps:

Prepare a brief research proposal (1–2 pages) outlining your topic and methodology. Email your CV and proposal to kapchits@example.com with the subject “Supervision Request.” Briefly describe your academic background, previous projects, and publication list (if any). Allow up to two weeks for a response; Dr. Kapchits conducts reviews every second Tuesday of the month.

Office location: Moscow, Leninsky Prospect 32, Building 3, Institute of Linguistics, Room 415. Office hours: Wednesdays 14:00–16:00 (MSK) or by appointment.

item.title: Teaching Materials
item.description:
Teaching Materials Below are downloadable PDFs of lecture notes and methodology guides for students interested in Somali linguistics:

“Somali Phonetics: Lecture Notes” (PDF, 2003) “Fieldwork Methods in African Linguistics” (PDF, 2007) “Somali Grammar Exercises” (PDF, 2010)

item.title: User Agreement
item.description:
User Agreement Terms of Use

Acceptance of Terms By accessing kapchits.online (“Site”), you agree to these terms. If you disagree, please do not use this Site. Intellectual Property All content on this Site, including but not limited to text, images, PDFs, and videos, is the property of Dr. Georgy L. Kapchits or its licensors and is protected by copyright laws. You may not reproduce, distribute, or create derivative works without express written permission. Permitted Use You may view, download, and print content for personal, non-commercial use. For any other use, you must obtain prior written consent. Disclaimer of Warranties The Site is provided “as is” without warranties of any kind. We do not guarantee the accuracy or completeness of the information provided. Limitation of Liability In no event shall Dr. Kapchits or its affiliates be liable for any indirect, incidental, or consequential damages arising out of your use of the Site. Links to Third-Party Sites This Site may contain links to third-party websites. We do not endorse or take responsibility for their content or practices. Governing Law These terms shall be governed by and construed in accordance with Russian Federation law, without regard to conflict of law principles. Changes to Terms We reserve the right to modify these terms at any time. Changes will be posted on this page with a “Last Updated” date.

item.title: Videos
item.description:
Video Lectures Below are selected video lectures and seminars by Dr. Kapchits on Somali linguistics. Click to watch:

“Introduction to Somali Phonology” – Seminar at MSU, 2019. [YouTube Embed Code or Link] “Nominal Morphology in Somali” – University of Helsinki, 2018. [YouTube Embed Code or Link] “Proverbs and Oral Tradition” – Moscow, 2020. [YouTube Embed Code or Link]
